humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLM performance on translating and summarizing 16th-century correspondence (history of Zurich reformation) written in historical languages (Latin and Early New High German).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluations are performed purely on textual inputs of historical letters.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Translation of historical Latin and Early New High German into modern German/English, and paragraph-by-paragraph cross-lingual summarization of correspondence.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: The evaluation focuses specifically on domain-specific translation and summarization of historical correspondence.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-4 in comparison with traditional online translation APIs.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The generative LLM (GPT-4) is the primary system under evaluation.

arts_humanities_data.note: Uses 16th-century letters from the Heinrich Bullinger correspondence, matching History and Language and Linguistics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses BLEU and ChrF for automatic evaluation, and structured human preference scores for quality assessment.

english_language.note: The paper and prompt designs are written in English.

summary: This paper presents a systematic evaluation of GPT-4 for machine translation and cross-language summarization of 16th-century letters written in Latin and Early New High German. By comparing GPT-4 against traditional translation engines, the authors demonstrate the model's significant superiority in processing historical languages. Additionally, the paper explores the viability of using paragraph-level summaries as a reference for translation and conducts a detailed human evaluation of automatic summarization quality.
key_findings: GPT-4 outperforms Google Translate by nearly 10 BLEU points on Latin-to-German (scoring 27.07 BLEU) and Latin-to-English (scoring 34.50 BLEU) translation. Human evaluators rated GPT-4's paragraph summaries highly for capturing times, events, and names (scoring up to 54.5 out of 58 for events) with zero observed hallucinations.
